revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従五位]][[ko:종5위]][[zh:從五位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従五位]][[ko:종5위]][[zh:從五位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従五位]][[ko:종5위]][[zh:從五位]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:従五位]][[ko:종5위]][[zh:從五位]]
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Jūgoi''' (従五位) is one of the {{ill|Court Ranks in Japan|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ranks of Japan|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|qid=Q11591025|lt=Shinkai (divine rank)|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Senior Fifth Rank|ja|正五位|1=Senior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|qq=}} and above {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}.<ref>https://www.wakapoetry.net/background/society/court-offices-titles-and-Ranks/court-Ranks/</ref> In the case of posthumous Ranks, it is referred to as Zō Jūgoi (贈従五位). In Japan's pre-modern Rank system, individuals holding the Rank of Jūgoi or higher were regarded as part of the '''{{ill|Nobility|en|Nobility|qid=Q134737|lt=Nobility of Japan|1=Nobility}}'''. The term Jūgoi was also used as an alternative title for the legitimate sons of {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|qid=Q241734|lt=Kazoku|1=Kazoku}}, as they were typically conferred this Rank.
== Before the Edo period ==
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}}, the Fifth Rank corresponded to {{ill|Kyōkan|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōkan|1=Central officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10883145}}, specifically the {{ill|Handan|ja|判官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handan|1=jou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1208615}} among the {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} of the {{ill|Eight Ministries|ja|八省|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ministries|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8193883}}. In the case of provincial officials, it was equivalent to {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} or generals of the {{ill|Chinjufu|ja|鎮守府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinjufu|1=Q11651159|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651159}}. Individuals holding the Rank of Jūgoi or higher were referred to as "Tsūki" (通貴), while those of {{ill|Jūsani|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūsani|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}} or higher were called "Ki" (貴), indicating the Rank's significance as the threshold for nobility. Additionally, Jūgoi was alternately known as "Eijaku" (栄爵), and those holding the Rank were called "Taifu" (大夫). In China, during the reign of the {{ill|Qin Shi Huang|en|Qin Shi Huang|qid=Q7192|lt=First Emperor of Qin|1=Qin Shi Huang}}, the Rank equivalent to Jūgoi was conferred upon pine trees, leading to the Japanese custom of calling this Rank "Matsu no Kurai" (松の位) or "Shōshaku" (松爵). Conferring this Rank was known as {{ill|Jojaku|ja|叙爵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jojaku|1=Honorary Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410718}}, and holding it was a qualification for {{ill|Heian nobility|ja|平安貴族|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian nobility|1=Heian aristocracy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11482408}}.
Individuals holding Jūgoi but serving in positions lower than the Sixth Rank, such as chamberlains in the {{ill|Saemonfu|ja|衛門府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saemonfu|1=Emonfu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11626338}}, were given special titles such as "Saemon Taifu" or "Uemon Taifu," signifying their unique status.
Prominent holders of this Rank included members of mid-Ranking noble families such as the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}}, {{ill|Tachibana clan (kuge)|en|Tachibana clan (kuge)|qid=Q1500519|lt=Tachibana clan|1=Tachibana clan (kuge)}}, {{ill|Takashina clan|ja|高階氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashina clan|1=Takashina clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673216}}, and various branches of military aristocracy, including the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|qid=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} and the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|桓武平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 平氏}}.
== After the Meiji period ==
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, the legitimate sons of the {{ill|Kazoku|en|Kazoku|qid=Q241734|lt=Kazoku|1=Kazoku}} were automatically conferred Jūgoi, which also became synonymous with the Rank itself. Military officers such as lieutenant colonels were also appointed to this Rank. From the late Edo period to the Taishō era, individuals who had contributed significantly to various fields, such as samurai during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu|1=Bakumatsu}} or those without Rank in earlier times, were occasionally awarded Jūgoi posthumously. The mathematician {{ill|Ajima Naonobu|en|Ajima Naonobu|qid=Q412791|lt=Ashima Naomoto|1=Ajima Naonobu}} is one such example.
Since May 3, 1946, appointments to this Rank have been restricted to posthumous cases. Individuals conferred this Rank include former chairs of {{ill|Prefectural councils in Japan|ja|都道府県議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural councils in Japan|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本の地方議会|1=local assembly in Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11505876}}, school principals, police chiefs, fire chiefs, CEOs, and contributors to sports and culture.
== Notable figures posthumously awarded Jūgoi under the Constitution of Japan == === Politicians === {{div col|cols=3}} * [[Takekoshi Tokuzō]] – Member of the House of Councillors * [[Yamada Mitsuo]] – Former Mayor of Matsuyama City * [[Ishikawa Rensaburō]] – Former Member of the House of Representatives {{div col end}}
=== Cultural figures === {{div col|cols=3}} * [[Takahashi Kyokutarō]] – Lyricist * [[Nakamura Shigeru (broadcaster)]] – NHK announcer * [[Katsura Bunji]] – Rakugo storyteller {{div col end}}
== References ==
* Tajiri Sa. *Zōi Shoken Den* (増補版 上), Kondo Publishing, 1975.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Shakujaku|ja|氏爵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakujaku|1=Q11547816|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11547816}} * {{ill|Nenjakujaku|ja|年爵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nenjakujaku|1=nenshaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483866}} * {{ill|Myōbu|en|Myōbu|qid=Q1036944|lt=Meifujaku|1=Myōbu}}
== External links ==
* [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] – {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/e-Gov Law Search|qq=}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] – National Diet Library Digital Archive {{Court Ranks of Japan}} *
